planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in early spring or fall; in zone 8–9 plant September–March. Diana Clare establishes best with consistent moisture during the first growing season. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in fall after foliage declines to maintain vigor. In zone 9 provide reliable shade and moisture to protect from summer heat; mulch heavily around the crown. The plant goes summer-dormant in heat — do not overwater dormant plants.

directions: Plant in full shade to light dappled shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Diana Clare is a highly awarded Pulmonaria cultivar from Terra Nova Nurseries, distinguished by its extraordinarily silver-splashed leaves — the foliage is so heavily overlaid with bright silver that individual leaves appear nearly entirely silver with just a hint of green remaining, creating one of the most dramatic foliage effects of any shade perennial. The plants bloom very early in spring (February–April) with vivid violet-blue to purple-red flowers (the classic lungwort two-tone pink-then-blue color shift as individual flowers age on the same plant) that are among the first nectar sources available to early pollinators including queen bumblebees emerging from winter dormancy. As the flowers fade the striking silver foliage takes over as the primary ornamental feature throughout summer and fall. Lungwort (Pulmonaria) is one of the most reliable low-maintenance shade groundcovers for the mid-Atlantic and Southeast, tolerating dry summer shade once established better than most shade perennials.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Pulmonaria Diana Clare Award of Garden Merit
Missouri Botanical Garden — Pulmonaria Plant Finder
North Carolina State University Extension — Shade Perennials for the Southeast

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 70°F; in zone 9 plant March–April for a spring crop and again July–August for fall. Lemon Cucumber is best harvested when pale yellow to ivory colored — fully yellow fruits are overripe and develop a slightly bitter skin. The round shape and distinctive color make it easy to spot on the vine when it's time to harvest. Plants are productive and compact enough for small trellises and can be grown in containers.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil with trellis support; Lemon Cucumber is an 1894 American heirloom producing distinctive round to oval, lemon-sized fruits with pale yellow-white skin when ripe — the resemblance to a lemon is strictly visual, as the flavor is mild, sweet, and refreshing with none of the tartness of actual lemon. The thin skin, small seeds, and crisp, sweet, very low-bitterness flesh make Lemon Cucumber one of the most palatable cucumbers for fresh eating without peeling — the skin remains tender and palatable throughout the eating-ripe stage (pale yellow to ivory) unlike the tough skins of slicing cucumbers. The round shape also makes Lemon Cucumber excellent for serving as a natural bowl — hollow out the round fruit and fill with dips, salads, or other fillings. The mild, sweet flavor and easy digestibility (lower cucurbitacin bitterness than many cucumbers) makes it popular for people who find regular cucumbers hard to digest.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Lemon Cucumber Heirloom History
Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Cucumber Variety Trial
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Lemon Cucumber Production

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is consistently warm; in zone 9 plant March–May and again August–September. October Bean is a climbing pole bean requiring a trellis, fence, or pole support 5–8 feet tall. The primary fresh-shelled eating stage (the classic Southern 'shellout') is when pods are plump and seeds are fully developed but still moist — at this stage they cook quickly and have a rich, creamy flavor. Pods allowed to dry fully can be stored and used as dry beans through winter.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil and provide sturdy pole or trellis support; October Bean is an Appalachian mountain heirloom shellout bean (also called 'fall bean', 'shelly bean', or 'October shellout') that has been grown in Southern Appalachian kitchen gardens continuously for generations — the bean is named for its traditional harvest period in September and October when the seeds have filled the pods but before autumn frosts dry the plants down. The large, tan to cream-colored, maroon-streaked seeds inside swollen pods are shelled and cooked fresh like a combination between fresh lima beans and dry beans — with a rich, meaty, potato-like texture and earthy, slightly sweet flavor quite different from green snap beans or fully dried beans. The freshly shelled October bean is a cornerstone of traditional Appalachian cooking, served with cornbread, potatoes, and green onions. The climbing vines produce abundantly over a long season, and any mature pods that escape the fresh-shelled harvest can be allowed to dry for a dried soup bean with excellent winter storage.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — October Bean Appalachian Heirloom
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — October Shellout Bean
University of Kentucky Cooperative Extension — Heirloom Beans of Appalachia

planting.note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost as soon as soil can be worked; in zone 9 sow November–February for a winter-spring harvest. Sugar Ann is a compact, bush-type snap pea requiring no staking — plants reach only 18–24 inches and are self-supporting. In zone 9 the fall-through-spring season is ideal; pods are ready to harvest in 52 days (faster than most snap peas) before spring heat causes the plants to decline. Pick pods when the peas have just begun to swell in the pod.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly acidic soil; Sugar Ann is the first snap pea cultivar to achieve a compact, self-supporting, bush habit — it was awarded the All-America Selections award in 1984 for this breakthrough characteristic, which made snap pea growing practical for small gardens and container growing where the typical 4–6 foot Sugar Snap vines require substantial trellis infrastructure. The pods are genuine snap peas (edible pod, no strings in the eating-ripe stage) with sweet, crunchy, crisp pod walls and rounded, sweet peas inside — the classic snap pea flavor that has made snap peas one of the most popular garden vegetables since their introduction in the late 1970s. At only 52–56 days to maturity, Sugar Ann is also the fastest-maturing of the main snap pea varieties, providing an early crop before hot weather ends the pea season. In zone 9 the bush habit and fast maturity make Sugar Ann particularly valuable for the short cool-season window available.
sources: All-America Selections — Sugar Ann Snap Pea 1984 Award
Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Snap Pea Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — English and Snap Peas for Florida

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil is warm. In zone 9 start in January–February and transplant in March–April. Thai Hot plants become large and very productive in zone 9's long warm season and often overwinter as woody perennials, returning to bear even heavier crops in subsequent seasons. Plants are ornamentally attractive when laden with clusters of upright red and green fruits in different stages of ripeness.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Thai Hot (also called Thai Bird Pepper or 'prik kee noo' in Thai, meaning 'mouse droppings pepper') is the standard small, intensely hot pepper used in authentic Thai, Vietnamese, Lao, and broader Southeast Asian cuisines. The thin, pointed, upright fruits (1–2 inches long, pencil-thin) ripen from bright green through red, with clusters of fruits in different ripening stages creating an ornamental display. The heat level of 50,000–100,000 Scoville Heat Units produces the sharp, penetrating, instant heat characteristic of Thai cuisine — different in character from the deeper, slower-building heat of dried Mexican chiles. Both green (unripe) and red (ripe) fruits are used in Thai cooking — green fruits appear in green curry paste, papaya salad, and stir-fries; red fruits are used in red curry paste and dried for chile flakes. The highly prolific plants bear continuously through warm weather, producing far more peppers than most home cooks can use fresh.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hot Pepper Production for Florida
New Mexico State University Chile Pepper Institute — Asian Hot Pepper Varieties
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Specialty and Ethnic Peppers
